name: Product Freeze Frame
description: Product UI freezes mid-motion, zooms in slightly (1→1.06 scale over 600ms), and a spotlight highlight sweeps left-to-right across the surface. Creates a cinematic 'hold for applause' beat that lets the audience absorb the product.

sweepWidthPercent.description: Width of the spotlight sweep as percentage of element width

sweepDelayMs.description: Delay before sweep begins (after zoom settles)

holdDurationMs.description: Static hold at end before next scene

properties.selector: string — CSS selector or layer ID for the element to freeze
properties.alt_text: string — Accessible description of what is being highlighted

sub_primitives.name: Freeze Zoom
sub_primitives.description: Smooth scale-up from 1 to target with deceleration easing. 600ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes freeze-zoom { from { transform: scale(1) } to { transform: scale(1.06) } }

sub_primitives.name: Spotlight Sweep
sub_primitives.description: Linear gradient highlight translates across element left-to-right. 900ms.

when_to_use: Hero product shot — pausing on the UI to let the audience absorb it
Post-demo beat — after showing a feature, freeze to emphasize the result
Transition anchor — freeze before a scene change to create a visual bookmark
when_to_avoid: Fast-paced montage sections — the hold kills momentum
Multiple freeze frames in one sequence — loses impact with repetition
Scenes under 2 seconds — needs time for zoom + sweep + hold
ai_guidance: Use as the 'pause for effect' moment after a product demo or feature reveal. Place at the emotional peak of a scene — not at the beginning. The sweep is subtle; resist the urge to increase sweepOpacity above 0.2 or it reads as a lens flare gimmick.

name: Zoom Through
description: Camera pushes through a UI element as it scales past frame edges, revealing the next layer behind. Creates a spatial transition that feels like diving into the interface — the foreground element grows beyond the viewport while the background layer fades up.

throughScale.description: Scale factor when foreground exits frame

revealDelayMs.description: Delay before background layer begins fading in

revealDurationMs.description: Duration of the background reveal fade

blurAmount.description: Blur applied to foreground as it scales past (simulates DOF)

focalPoint.description: Transform origin for zoom: center, top-left, top-right, etc.

properties.focal_selector: string — Optional sub-element to center the zoom on

properties.layer_id: string — Layer revealed behind the foreground

sub_primitives.name: Scale Through
sub_primitives.description: Element scales from 1 to 3.5x while gaining blur, exiting viewport. 800ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes scale-through { from { transform: scale(1); filter: blur(0) } to { transform: scale(3.5); filter: blur(8px) } }

sub_primitives.name: Layer Reveal
sub_primitives.description: Background layer fades from 0 to 1 opacity. 500ms.

when_to_use: Spatial navigation — drilling from overview into a detail view
Scene transitions that imply depth and layers in a product
Montage sequences where speed and energy need a dramatic push
when_to_avoid: Editorial personality — the blur and 3D depth violate editorial rules
Sequences with more than 2 zoom-throughs — creates nausea
Scenes where the background layer isn't ready to be revealed yet
ai_guidance: This is a transition-as-hero-moment. Use it when moving between two related views where the spatial relationship matters (e.g., dashboard → detail panel). The focalPoint config is critical — zoom into the element the user would naturally look at. Avoid center zoom unless the content is symmetrical.

name: Metric Explosion
description: A number counts up rapidly (odometer style), then 'pops' at its final value with a radial particle burst and brief glow pulse. Designed for hero stats — revenue numbers, user counts, performance metrics — where the number itself is the star.

countUpDurationMs.description: Duration of the number counting phase

countUpEasing.description: Easing for count-up (decelerates toward final value)

particleRadius.description: Max radius particles travel from center

glowColor.description: Color of the glow pulse (inherits text color by default)

sub_primitives.name: Count Up
sub_primitives.description: JS-driven number interpolation from 0 to target value with eased timing. 1600ms.

sub_primitives.name: Value Pop
sub_primitives.description: Scale overshoot (1→1.12→1) with spring settle at final value. 300ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes value-pop { 0% { transform: scale(1) } 50% { transform: scale(1.12) } 100% { transform: scale(1) } }

sub_primitives.name: Radial Burst
sub_primitives.description: 12 particles radiate outward from center with fade-out. JS required for dynamic positioning. 600ms.

sub_primitives.name: Glow Pulse
sub_primitives.description: Box-shadow glow expands and fades. 400ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes glow-pulse { 0% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 currentColor } 50% { box-shadow: 0 0 20px 4px currentColor } 100% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 transparent } }

when_to_use: Hero stat reveal — the single most important number in a pitch or demo
Growth metrics in investor decks — ARR, user count, performance gains
Dashboard reveals where a KPI is the punchline
when_to_avoid: Multiple metrics in sequence — pick one hero number, show the rest quietly
Small or unimpressive numbers — the explosion amplifies the number, so it must be worth it
Neutral-light personality — particles and glow are too theatrical for tutorials
ai_guidance: This is the 'mic drop' number. Reserve it for the single most impressive metric in a sequence. The count-up builds tension and the pop delivers the payoff. If you need to show multiple stats, use metric-explosion for the hero number and a simpler fade-in for supporting stats. The glowColor should match the brand or data color, not be arbitrary.

name: Card Fan Out
description: Stacked cards spread into a fan arrangement, revealing all options simultaneously. Cards begin in a centered stack and rotate outward to evenly-spaced angles while translating apart, creating a hand-of-cards reveal that shows breadth of options.

cardCount.description: Number of cards in the fan (3–7 recommended)

fanAngle.description: Total spread angle in degrees (divided among cards)

fanRadius.description: Radius of the fan arc in pixels

spreadEasing.description: Easing with slight overshoot for organic feel

staggerMs.description: Stagger delay between each card's spread start

liftY.description: Y offset during spread (cards rise slightly)

sub_primitives.name: Stack Spread
sub_primitives.description: Cards rotate and translate from stacked center to fan positions. JS required for per-card angle calculation. 1200ms.

sub_primitives.name: Card Lift
sub_primitives.description: Subtle Y translation during spread for organic arc feel. 1200ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes card-lift { from { transform: translateY(0) } 50% { transform: translateY(-20px) } to { transform: translateY(0) } }

sub_primitives.name: Highlight Pulse
sub_primitives.description: Optional border/shadow pulse on the highlighted card after fan settles. 400ms.

when_to_use: Feature comparison — showing multiple plan tiers or product options at once
Portfolio reveal — displaying a range of capabilities simultaneously
Options/choices moment — 'here are your paths forward'
when_to_avoid: More than 7 cards — fan becomes unreadable and cards overlap
Cards with dense content — the rotated angle makes text hard to read
Sequences needing fast pacing — the 2s duration is a deliberate pause
ai_guidance: The fan is a reveal of breadth — use it when you want the audience to see 'look at all these options.' Mark at most one card as highlight: true to draw attention after the fan settles. For editorial personality, keep fanAngle under 30 degrees for a more restrained spread.

name: Chart to Insight Reveal
description: Chart data animates in (bars grow, lines draw, points plot), then a callout annotation flies in pointing to the key insight with a connector line. Two-phase primitive: first the data tells the story, then the annotation delivers the punchline.

chartType.description: Chart type: bar, line, donut, area

calloutDelayMs.description: Gap between chart build completion and callout entry

calloutDirection.description: Direction callout enters from: left, right, top, bottom

properties.data_points: array — Numeric values for the chart
properties.labels: array — Labels for each data point
properties.highlight_index: number — Index of the data point the callout points to

properties.text: string — Insight text (e.g., '3x growth in Q4')

sub_primitives.name: Chart Build
sub_primitives.description: Data elements animate in sequence — bars grow from baseline, lines draw with stroke-dashoffset, points scale in. JS required for per-element orchestration. 1400ms.

sub_primitives.name: Callout Fly-In
sub_primitives.description: Annotation card slides in from configured direction with fade. 500ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes callout-fly-in { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(40px) } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateX(0) } }

sub_primitives.name: Connector Draw
sub_primitives.description: SVG line draws from callout to data point using stroke-dashoffset. 300ms.

when_to_use: Data storytelling — showing a chart then highlighting the key takeaway
Investor pitch decks — metrics that need a 'so what?' annotation
Product analytics features — demonstrating insight discovery
when_to_avoid: Charts with no clear insight — if there's no punchline, just build the chart
Scenes under 3 seconds — the two-phase animation needs breathing room
Montage pacing — too slow and detailed for rapid-cut sequences
ai_guidance: This is data storytelling in motion. The chart build is act one (the evidence), the callout is act two (the insight). The highlight_index must point to the most interesting data point — don't annotate the obvious. For editorial personality, this is a natural fit. For cinematic-dark, increase contrast and add a subtle glow to the highlighted bar/point.

name: Logo Resolve
description: Logo elements assemble from separate parts — icon and wordmark arrive independently with spring physics, then settle into final lockup position. The icon lands first with a bounce, the wordmark slides into alignment, and a subtle scale-settle confirms the resolve.

iconEntryDirection.description: Direction icon enters from: top, bottom, left, right

wordmarkDelayMs.description: Delay between icon settle and wordmark entry

resolveScale.description: Slight scale overshoot before settling to 1

properties.icon_src: string — Path or URL to icon/mark asset
properties.wordmark_src: string — Path or URL to wordmark asset (or text)
properties.lockup: string — Final arrangement: horizontal, vertical, stacked (default: horizontal)

sub_primitives.name: Icon Spring Land
sub_primitives.description: Icon enters from configured direction and lands with spring physics bounce. JS spring simulation required. 800ms.

sub_primitives.name: Wordmark Slide
sub_primitives.description: Wordmark translates into position alongside icon with deceleration. 600ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes wordmark-slide { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(30px) } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateX(0) } }

sub_primitives.name: Lockup Settle
sub_primitives.description: Complete logo scales from 1.02 to 1 for a satisfying micro-settle. 300ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes lockup-settle { from { transform: scale(1.02) } to { transform: scale(1) } }

when_to_use: Video endings — the final logo moment before fade to black
Brand reveals in sizzle reels — logo as the climactic resolve
Opening sequences where brand identity needs a premium entrance
when_to_avoid: Mid-sequence logo appearances — this is an opener or closer, not a watermark
Editorial personality — spring physics feel too theatrical for content-forward work
Sequences with multiple brand logos — this is for the primary brand only
ai_guidance: This is the logo's hero moment — typically the last 2-3 seconds of a video or the opening brand reveal. The spring physics on the icon are critical for premium feel; don't replace with CSS easing. For montage, increase stiffness (220+) for snappier landing. Always pair with a clean background — no competing elements.

name: Before-After Morph
description: Two states crossfade with a sliding divider line, morphing between before and after views. A vertical divider sweeps across the frame — content on the left shows the 'before' state, content on the right reveals the 'after' state. Classic comparison pattern elevated with smooth animation.

dividerStartPercent.description: Starting position of divider (percentage from left)

dividerWidth.description: Width of the divider line in pixels

properties.layer_id: string — Layer or image for the 'before' state
properties.label: string (optional) — Custom label, defaults to 'Before'

properties.layer_id: string — Layer or image for the 'after' state
properties.label: string (optional) — Custom label, defaults to 'After'

sub_primitives.name: Divider Sweep
sub_primitives.description: Vertical divider line translates from left to right across the frame. 2000ms.

sub_primitives.name: Clip Reveal
sub_primitives.description: After-state clip-path expands in sync with divider position. 2000ms.
sub_primitives.css: @keyframes clip-reveal { from { clip-path: inset(0 95% 0 0) } to { clip-path: inset(0 5% 0 0) } }

sub_primitives.name: Label Fade
sub_primitives.description: Before/After labels fade in at start and end of sweep. 400ms.

when_to_use: Product transformation demos — showing old vs new UI, before vs after optimization
Design comparisons — wireframe to final, legacy to modern
Tutorial explanations — showing the effect of a setting or configuration
when_to_avoid: States that are too similar — the morph needs visible contrast to be meaningful
Montage pacing — 3 seconds is too slow for rapid-cut sequences
Content where before/after framing is misleading or reductive
ai_guidance: The before-after morph is persuasion through contrast. The sweep speed controls how much time the audience spends comparing — faster sweep for obvious differences, slower for subtle ones. For editorial, use a thin divider (1-2px) and muted labels. For neutral-light tutorials, thicker divider and prominent labels help comprehension.
